core.action: Use patient mode (skip buying when cash is insufficient) instead of ending episodes early — this provides more training samples and teaches agents to wait for favorable capital conditions

consequence.description: Ending episodes prematurely wastes valuable training opportunities and may cause the agent to learn overly conservative position sizing to avoid capital depletion scenarios

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-047

core.when: When implementing discrete action spaces for stock trading backtesting
core.action: Use shares_increment=1 to restrict trades to whole share quantities — fractional shares may not be executable in real markets and this constraint ensures backtest-live consistency

consequence.description: Allowing fractional shares in backtesting creates execution inconsistency where backtested trades cannot be replicated in live trading, causing systematic overestimation of achievable returns

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-046

core.when: When implementing model validation for ensemble training in stock trading
core.action: Use 63-day validation window matching the rebalancing frequency — this ensures evaluation periods mirror actual deployment conditions and capture recent market regimes

consequence.description: Using shorter windows (e.g., 30 days) introduces noisy estimates causing suboptimal model selection, while longer windows (e.g., 126 days) evaluate on outdated market regimes leading to poor live performance

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-055

core.when: When designing or modifying data preprocessing pipelines for stock selection
core.action: Define explicit NaN handling strategy with regime detection (suspended vs trading periods) before applying forward-fill — differentiate between fillable gaps (temporary data gaps) and non-fillable gaps (delisted/suspended securities) to avoid artificial price continuity

consequence.description: Forward-filling without regime detection creates fake price history for suspended or delisted stocks, producing incorrect technical indicators and causing the strategy to select or avoid stocks based on fabricated data

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-122

core.when: When designing or modifying stock universe filtering logic in preprocessing pipelines
core.action: Use a unified ticker quality threshold across each preprocessing stages — do not apply multiple independent ticker dropping rules with different thresholds as this creates inconsistent portfolio universe definitions

consequence.description: Inconsistent ticker removal across pipeline stages causes the same stock to be included or excluded depending on which preprocessing variant is used, making strategy comparisons unreliable and backtest results non-reproducible

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-123

core.when: When implementing or modifying commission/fee calculation logic in portfolio optimization
core.action: Use the TRF (Transaction Remainder Factor) fixed-point iteration model for computing transaction costs — do not replace with flat fee, closed-form approximation, or tiered structures without validating cost accuracy

consequence.description: Replacing TRF with simpler models changes transaction cost dynamics; strategies profitable under flat fees may become loss-making when realistic frictions are applied, causing backtest-live divergence

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-075

core.when: When implementing PPO reinforcement learning for trading agents
core.action: Implement clipped surrogate objective with GAE (Generalized Advantage Estimation) for policy updates — do not replace with vanilla policy gradient or TRPO without re-validating training stability

consequence.description: Using unclipped surrogate objectives or vanilla policy gradient risks destructive policy updates; training may diverge or produce unstable strategies that perform erratically in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-076

core.when: When implementing experience replay for portfolio optimization training
core.action: Store complete return trajectories with automatic eviction of old experiences — do not use prioritized replay or episodic memory structures without validating off-policy correction

consequence.description: Changing replay strategy alters off-policy learning dynamics; prioritized replay without proper correction may introduce bias, causing trained policies to underperform or become unstable

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-084

core.when: When converting policy logits to portfolio weight distributions
core.action: Apply softmax normalization to convert raw logits to portfolio weights ensuring sum equals 1 — do not use sigmoid with scaling or other methods that allow negative weights

consequence.description: Using sigmoid with scaling allows negative portfolio weights (short positions) or weights summing to non-1 values; strategies designed for long-only portfolios will malfunction when shorts are introduced unexpectedly

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-053

core.when: When implementing buy/sell order execution logic in stock trading environments
core.action: Validate that price > 0 before executing any trade; explicitly check for NaN, None, zero, and negative values and reject orders with invalid prices

consequence.description: Executing trades on invalid prices (zero, negative, NaN) causes incorrect position sizing and capital allocation, leading to financial losses and corrupted portfolio state that cannot be recovered

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-107

core.when: When implementing TRF commission fee calculations in portfolio optimization environments
core.action: Implement convergence checking for the mu calculation fixed-point iteration loop; verify mu has converged within acceptable tolerance before using the value in commission calculations

consequence.description: Using unconverged mu values in commission calculations introduces numerical instability that compounds over trading days, causing progressively larger fee calculation errors and portfolio value distortion

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-109

core.when: When implementing or refactoring Policy Gradient loss computation for portfolio optimization
core.action: Verify the loss function uses negative log-likelihood with gradient ASCENT (not descent); if entropy bonus is included, verify it is added, not subtracted

consequence.description: Using gradient descent instead of gradient ascent inverts the optimization direction, causing the policy to maximize bad actions rather than good ones; backtest results will show consistent losses

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-068

core.when: When implementing EI3 policy architecture for financial time series processing
core.action: Implement multi-scale convolutional filters capturing short-term momentum, medium-term trends, and long-term mean reversion simultaneously; verify filter kernel sizes match the intended temporal scales

consequence.description: Using single-scale convolutions or incorrect filter sizes fails to capture important temporal patterns, reducing strategy effectiveness across different market regimes and leading to poor backtest performance

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-072

core.when: When implementing advantage computation in policy gradient calculations
core.action: Normalize advantage values to zero mean and unit variance before policy gradient computation; verify normalization is applied after advantage calculation and before loss computation

consequence.description: Skipping advantage normalization causes gradient magnitudes to vary with market volatility, leading to unstable training in high-volatility periods and poor convergence to optimal policies

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-077

core.when: When implementing mean-variance portfolio optimization
core.action: Use EfficientFrontier.max_sharpe() for maximum Sharpe ratio optimization; verify expected returns and covariance matrix inputs are properly estimated from historical data before optimization

consequence.description: Using minimum variance instead of max Sharpe ratio changes the optimization objective fundamentally, producing overly conservative portfolios that don't match the intended risk-adjusted return objectives

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-079

core.when: When implementing multi-asset portfolio feature normalization in backtesting
core.action: Apply independent per-ticker normalization using GroupByScaler — features must be normalized within each asset's own historical range, not across each tickers simultaneously

consequence.description: Global cross-ticker normalization mixes price scales from different assets, causing features for low-priced stocks to be dwarfed by high-priced ones and leading to systematically biased feature representations that degrade model performance

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-082

core.when: When implementing return calculation in stock trading backtesting
core.action: Calculate returns as percentage change between consecutive prices — do not substitute with log returns or other return definitions without adjusting strategy logic accordingly

consequence.description: Switching to log returns changes strategy signal magnitudes and timing, particularly for high-volatility stocks where log returns systematically differ from percentage returns, causing backtest-live performance divergence

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-083

core.when: When implementing turbulence-based risk management in stock trading
core.action: Trigger risk-off selling behavior when turbulence index exceeds the defined threshold — the threshold value is a critical parameter that must remain consistent with the trained agent's learned behavior

consequence.description: Modifying the turbulence threshold without retraining causes the agent to switch risk modes at incorrect market stress levels, either overselling in mild conditions or failing to protect capital during actual market crashes

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-087

core.when: When implementing cash reserve management in trading environment
core.action: Apply cash penalty proportion for positions that do not maintain minimum cash reserves — verify the penalty discourages over-investment without being so severe that the agent avoids each positions

consequence.description: Removing or reducing the cash reserve penalty allows the agent to over-invest, leading to insufficient liquidity for new opportunities and potential forced liquidations at unfavorable prices during market gaps

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-089

core.when: When implementing portfolio state representation in portfolio optimization
core.action: Normalize temporal variations relative to the previous time window's value — verify the state representation captures relative changes rather than absolute magnitudes to maintain scale invariance

consequence.description: Changing temporal normalization to absolute values breaks scale invariance, causing the agent to produce different portfolio allocations for the same relative market movements at different absolute price levels, degrading generalization

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-090

core.when: When implementing order execution logic in StockTradingEnv.step()
core.action: Execute sell orders first before buy orders using argsort with sell_index < buy_index ordering — the argsort approach depends on this specific ordering to flatten short positions before opening new long positions

consequence.description: Changing execution order to buy-before-sell creates unintended leverage when capital is insufficient, potentially triggering margin cascade scenarios where new buy orders execute on borrowed capital before shorts are closed

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-100

core.when: When implementing turbulence threshold for live trading inference
core.action: Use the same turbulence threshold quantile (90th percentile) during live trading that was used during training — do not use 99th percentile or any other threshold value without retraining the agent

consequence.description: Training uses 90th percentile threshold (more cautious) while trading uses 99th percentile (less cautious), causing the agent to fail to activate risk-off behavior during actual turbulent market conditions learned in training, leading to large drawdowns during regime shifts

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-103

core.when: When implementing or verifying the reward function in the stock trading environment
core.action: Verify that the gamma parameter (discount factor) in the reward calculation matches the intended investment horizon; higher gamma values (e.g., 0.99-0.999) prioritize long-term returns while lower values (e.g., 0.9-0.95) incentivize short-term profits

consequence.description: Using a gamma value misaligned with investment horizon causes the DRL agent to optimize for the wrong time horizon; short-horizon agents generate excessive transactions while long-horizon agents may underreact to short-term signals

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-013

core.when: When preprocessing features for DRL stock trading models
core.action: Apply per-ticker feature scaling (normalizing each ticker's features to its own statistical range) rather than global scaling across each stocks; verify high-price stocks (e.g., BRK.A) do not dominate the state space over low-price stocks

consequence.description: Using global feature scaling causes high-price stocks to dominate the state representation, leading the DRL agent to allocate disproportionate attention to expensive stocks and underallocate to lower-priced equities, distorting learned trading policies

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-010

core.when: When implementing portfolio allocation policies that may transfer between PortfolioOptimizationEnv and StockTradingEnv
core.action: Assume identical policy architectures work across environments without modification — softmax normalization guarantees sum-to-one weights in PortfolioOptimizationEnv while Box space in StockTradingEnv allows arbitrary distributions

consequence.description: Transferring a policy trained with softmax outputs to StockTradingEnv causes invalid portfolio allocations since the environment expects unnormalized Box space actions, resulting in undefined behavior or gradient computation failures

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-106

core.when: When integrating training monitoring for Stable Baselines3 agents
core.action: Use TensorboardCallback from Stable Baselines3 for standardized training visualization — this callback logs scalar metrics and episode stats to tensorboard event files and is required for SB3-compatible agent training monitoring

consequence.description: Without proper TensorboardCallback integration, training metrics are not logged to tensorboard event files, making it impossible to visualize training progress, compare runs, or debug training issues using standard tensorboard tools

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-017

validation_threshold: TensorboardCallback not imported or not passed to model.train() → FAIL

core.when: When implementing risk management logic for market turbulence scenarios
core.action: Trigger forced liquidation of each positions when market turbulence exceeds the configured threshold — liquidation must execute at the next observation after detecting turbulence > threshold to prevent holding through correlated crash scenarios

consequence.description: Without turbulence-triggered liquidation, the strategy holds positions through market crashes, causing catastrophic losses as portfolio destruction occurs during correlated crisis events

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-014

validation_threshold: turbulence_threshold == None OR sell_all_turbulence not called when turbulence > threshold → FAIL

core.when: When implementing or modifying action space definitions for StockTradingEnv
core.action: Maintain continuous action space normalized to [-1, 1] range — this normalization is required because A2C/PPO/DDPG algorithms each support continuous Box space and discrete action requires internal mapping layer

consequence.description: Changing the action space from normalized [-1, 1] to algorithm-specific spaces would break compatibility with standard DRL algorithms, requiring separate environment implementations per algorithm

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-012

validation_threshold: action_space max_value > 1.0 OR action_space min_value < -1.0 → FAIL

core.when: When implementing multi-environment training or transferring policies between portfolio optimization and stock trading environments
core.action: Handle action space incompatibility explicitly — portfolio environment outputs softmax (sum-to-one weights) while stock trading uses Box space (unbounded); require environment-specific policy heads or action transformation layer to prevent policy transfer failures

consequence.description: Policies trained in portfolio environment produce softmax-transformed outputs incompatible with stock trading Box action space, causing training failures or silent policy degradation when transferring across environments

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-117

core.when: When implementing multi-algorithm DRL training pipeline with model selection
core.action: Implement ensemble strategy beyond single best selection — use weighted averaging, minimum-variance portfolio selection, or regime-aware model switching instead of argmax Sharpe selection to capture complementary model strengths during market regime shifts

consequence.description: Single best Sharpe selection discards complementary information from underperforming models; during regime shifts, previously inferior models (e.g., PPO in trending markets) become superior, and argmax selection cannot anticipate this, causing systematic underperformance

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-120

core.when: When implementing portfolio rebalancing logic in backtesting
core.action: Rebalance only at unique trade dates within training window boundaries — this prevents look-ahead bias by ensuring rebalancing occurs only at window boundaries, forcing the agent to maintain portfolio state between rebalances

consequence.description: Continuous rebalancing creates unrealistic transaction-heavy backtest results; not enforcing window-boundary rebalancing allows look-ahead bias where the agent accesses future information to adjust positions mid-window

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-020

validation_threshold: rebalance_frequency != 'window_boundary' OR trade_dates contains future_date → FAIL

core.when: When implementing risk control logic using turbulence indicators
core.action: Compute turbulence threshold from training period distribution only (90th percentile quantile); use this threshold at each rebalance point to determine risk-on/risk-off regime — do not use future turbulence data for threshold computation

consequence.description: Computing threshold from full dataset including future data causes look-ahead bias in regime detection; the risk control switch will appear more accurate in backtesting than in live trading where only historical data is available

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-021

core.when: When implementing or extending DRL agent training code
core.action: Maintain the library-agnostic interface by implementing get_model, train_model, and DRL_prediction methods for each agent; use conditional imports to support multiple libraries without modifying existing training scripts

consequence.description: Breaking the library-agnostic interface by using direct library imports forces refactoring of all training scripts when switching algorithms, increasing maintenance burden and preventing algorithm comparison experiments

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-016

core.when: When implementing execution logic for order processing
core.action: Maintain strict separation between test mode (using historical data only) and trade mode (requiring API credentials and executing real orders) — never allow a unified execution path

consequence.description: Unified execution paths risk executing live trades during development, causing real financial losses from untested order generation logic

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-022

core.when: When implementing position liquidation logic in backtesting or live trading
core.action: Execute sell orders in argsort order (worst performers first) — sort positions by return in ascending order and sell the lowest performers first

consequence.description: Changing to sell best performers first fundamentally alters the strategy from mean-reversion to momentum, causing the backtest to reflect a different trading hypothesis and producing non-reproducible live results

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-041

core.when: When implementing turbulence-based risk management logic
core.action: Use turbulence threshold of 70 to trigger risk-off mode — above this threshold, liquidate each positions to avoid tail risk during market stress

consequence.description: Removing or modifying the turbulence threshold disables automatic risk-off mode, causing the strategy to hold positions during high-volatility periods when covariance matrices become unstable and tail risk is elevated

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-035

core.when: When configuring training parameters for reinforcement learning models
core.action: Use preset training timesteps of 20,000 (quick demo) or 50,000 (serious training) — do not use arbitrary timestep values outside these presets without empirical validation

consequence.description: Arbitrary timestep values may cause underfitting (too few) or wasted computation with no convergence benefit (too many), producing models that either underperform or consume excessive resources

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-039

core.when: When using the framework's default training timestep parameter for model training
core.action: Verify that the default training timesteps (20K or 50K) match the expected convergence requirements for the specific strategy and environment complexity

consequence.description: Default timesteps of 20K (demo) and 50K (production) were derived empirically for general environments; strategies with higher-dimensional action spaces or more complex reward functions may require more training, resulting in undertrained models

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-039

core.when: When implementing portfolio weight calculation in FinRL portfolio optimization
core.action: Apply softmax function to normalize portfolio weights to verify they form a valid probability distribution (sum to 1, respect long/short constraints)

consequence.description: Using hard normalization (divide by sum) or projection methods instead of softmax produces invalid weight distributions that violate long/short constraints, leading to incorrect position sizing and degraded policy performance

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-074

core.action: Verify EfficientFrontier parameters (risk_aversion, weight_bounds) match the actual trading universe and broker fee structure before using MVO as performance baseline

consequence.description: MVO baseline using default EfficientFrontier parameters may not match actual market conditions, causing misleading performance comparisons where DRL appears to add or lose value when it actually does not

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-049

core.action: Include DJIA (^DJI) benchmark performance in backtest reporting to quantify alpha generation — strategy should at minimum match or exceed DJIA returns to justify active management costs

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-050

core.action: Use time_window=50 (approximately 2 months of daily data) for the EIIE convolutional encoder — do not change to arbitrary values without validating pattern recognition capability

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-051

core.when: When implementing or refactoring turbulence index calculation using Mahalanobis distance
core.action: Replace the pandas .cov() covariance matrix with exponentially weighted covariance, shrinkage estimators, or any other covariance estimator without adjusting the turbulence threshold parameters — different estimators produce materially different covariance structures that change regime detection

consequence.description: Substituting a different covariance estimator changes the eigenvalue distribution of the covariance matrix, causing the Mahalanobis distance-based turbulence index to detect different market stress levels and potentially triggering false regime switches

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-095

core.when: When implementing position management and risk control logic in backtesting
core.action: Modify the stop-loss threshold from 90% of purchase price or change the profit/loss ratio from 2 — these values define the fundamental risk-reward profile of the trading strategy

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-088

core.action: Modify the epsilon exploration parameter from its empirically tuned value without re-evaluating convergence behavior and final policy performance

consequence.description: Epsilon values that are too high cause random exploration to dominate, preventing the agent from learning meaningful trading patterns; values too low cause premature convergence to suboptimal policies, both leading to significantly degraded backtest performance

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-091

core.when: When implementing GPM policy network and action selection in portfolio optimization
core.action: Change the softmax temperature parameter from its calibrated value without validating the resulting action distribution matches expected policy behavior

consequence.description: Temperature values that are too low produce near-deterministic policies that overfit to specific market conditions; values too high produce uniform random exploration that ignores learned patterns, both causing backtest-live inconsistency

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-092

core.when: When implementing or modifying the reward function for portfolio optimization
core.action: Replace log returns with simple returns, Sharpe ratio, or Sortino ratio as the reward signal without updating each portfolio performance calculations — log returns are additive under continuous compounding, while simple returns are not

consequence.description: Using simple returns as reward introduces path-dependent compounding errors where the reward signal does not accurately represent portfolio growth, causing the RL agent to optimize for a metric that diverges from actual accumulated returns over time

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-094

core.when: When implementing turbulence-based risk management in production trading
core.action: Use a fixed turbulence threshold (e.g., 70) in production that differs from the training distribution — training uses 90th quantile while trading uses fixed threshold, creating systematic under-protection during market stress

consequence.description: Training classifies more states as turbulent (90th percentile) than live trading (fixed 70), causing the agent to learn conservative risk-off behavior that underactivates in production; during volatile periods, the agent expects turbulence-based exits to trigger earlier than they will, potentially allowing excessive drawdowns before risk controls engage

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-115

core.when: When computing TRF commission fees using fixed-point iteration for mu values
core.action: Implement explicit convergence checking for fixed-point iteration before using mu values in commission calculations — verify iteration has converged or fall back to conservative estimates

consequence.description: Without convergence checking, non-converged mu values produce incorrect commission estimates that accumulate across trades; for high-turnover strategies or volatile positions, unconverged iterations systematically underestimate costs by 5-20%, inflating apparent strategy profitability and creating regulatory compliance risk

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-118

core.when: When running hyperparameter search with Ray Tune in distributed or shared cluster environments
core.action: Verify and explicitly configure max_failures to a non-zero value (recommended: 3 with exponential backoff) if trial failures are expected due to resource contention, before starting long-running hyperparameter tuning jobs

consequence.description: Default max_failures=0 causes the entire hyperparameter search to stall indefinitely when any trial encounters expected failures (e.g., OOM, resource busy), wasting hours of compute time on shared clusters

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-001

core.action: Configure checkpoint_freq to a positive integer value (e.g., every 10-20 iterations) before starting long training runs, to enable recovery from interruptions without losing significant progress

consequence.description: Default checkpoint_freq=0 means no intermediate saves occur; a cluster interruption or crash after 100+ iterations loses all training progress since the last explicit checkpoint, requiring full restart

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-002

core.when: When scaling from single-worker laptop training to multi-worker distributed training
core.action: Explicitly set init_ray=True in each entry point scripts that may trigger distributed training, rather than relying on the default behavior; document the flag requirement in deployment runbooks

consequence.description: Default init_ray=False silently disables distributed training capabilities; multi-worker training scripts fail to spawn additional workers without the explicit flag, causing distributed training attempts to fall back to single-worker mode without warning

evidence_refs.summary: 220f9e4 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-005

core.when: When ingesting market data from multiple sources (Alpaca and Yahoo Finance) into backtesting pipelines
core.action: Implement source-specific data validation and normalization before merging datasets: standardize column names (close/adj_close), verify timestamp alignment (timezone, frequency), validate OHLCV relationships (high >= low, volume >= 0), and log data source provenance for audit trails

consequence.description: Without source-specific validation, column name mismatches (e.g., Alpaca uses 'close' vs Yahoo 'adj_close') or timezone offsets silently corrupt merged datasets, causing strategies to trade on misaligned price data and producing incorrect backtest results
